import pandas as pd
import hvplot.pandas
import panel as pn
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import Point, LineString, Polygon
from matplotlib.cm import Reds, Blues, Greens
import cartopy.crs as ccrs
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
from bokeh.resources import INLINE
# load data 

the_data = gpd.read_file("../data/indexed_data_polygons.gpkg")
gdf = the_data.copy()
gdf = gdf.set_crs("EPSG:28992", allow_override=True)
gdf = gdf.reset_index(drop=True)
#gdf["geometry"] = gdf["geometry"].simplify(tolerance=1000, preserve_topology=True)
sectors_ = ["third_places_total_ranked", 
            "third_places_non_medical_total_ranked",
            "third_places_non_medical_non_retail_total_ranked",
            "third_places_medical_ranked",
            "third_places_food_beverage_ranked",
            "third_places_retail_ranked",
            "third_places_community_ranked",
            "third_places_cultural_ranked",
            "third_places_sport_leisure_ranked"]

LABELS = {
    "All Third Places": "third_places_total_ranked",
    "All Third Places except Medical Places": "third_places_non_medical_total_ranked",
    "All Third Places except Medical and Retail Places": "third_places_non_medical_non_retail_total_ranked",
    "Medical Places only": "third_places_medical_ranked",
    "Food and Beverage only": "third_places_food_beverage_ranked",
    "Retail Places only": "third_places_retail_ranked",
    "Community Places only": "third_places_community_ranked",
    "Cultural Places only": "third_places_cultural_ranked",
    "Sport and Leisure only": "third_places_sport_leisure_ranked",
}
pn.extension(design="material", sizing_mode="stretch_width")
def blend_with_white(base_color, t):
    """
    t in [0, 1]
    0 -> white
    1 -> full base_color
    """
    base = np.array(mcolors.to_rgb(base_color))
    white = np.array([1, 1, 1])
    rgb = (1 - t) * white + t * base
    return mcolors.to_hex(rgb)
print(gdf["stedelijkheid"].isna().sum())
print(gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"].isna().sum())
0
21
gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"][np.isnan(gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"])] = 0
/var/folders/_r/0g6wk2g978v89dhggxd0jbcm0000gn/T/ipykernel_90522/3880200520.py:1: FutureWarning: ChainedAssignmentError: behaviour will change in pandas 3.0!
You are setting values through chained assignment. Currently this works in certain cases, but when using Copy-on-Write (which will become the default behaviour in pandas 3.0) this will never work to update the original DataFrame or Series, because the intermediate object on which we are setting values will behave as a copy.
A typical example is when you are setting values in a column of a DataFrame, like:

df["col"][row_indexer] = value

Use `df.loc[row_indexer, "col"] = values` instead, to perform the assignment in a single step and ensure this keeps updating the original `df`.

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy

  gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"][np.isnan(gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"])] = 0
/var/folders/_r/0g6wk2g978v89dhggxd0jbcm0000gn/T/ipykernel_90522/3880200520.py:1: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"][np.isnan(gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"])] = 0
# one base color per category
cats = sorted(gdf["stedelijkheid"].dropna().unique())
palette = plt.get_cmap("tab10").colors

cat_to_color = {
    1: "#023858",  # dark blue
    2: "#0570b0",  # blue
    3: "#addd8e",  # green
    4: "#238443",  # orange
    5: "#004529",  # red
}

#cat_to_color = {cat: palette[i % len(palette)] for i, cat in enumerate(cats)}

# normalize numeric sector to 0..1
s = gdf["third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation"].astype(float)
s_norm = (s - s.min()) / (s.max() - s.min() + 1e-9)

# combine category hue + numeric intensity
# low values -> lighter, high values -> stronger
gdf["facecolor"] = [
    blend_with_white(cat_to_color[cat], 0.25 + 0.75 * val)
    for cat, val in zip(gdf["stedelijkheid"], s_norm)
]
def create_map(sector = "third_places_total_ranked"):
    return gdf.hvplot.polygons(
        c=sector,
        geo=True,
        crs=ccrs.epsg(28992),
        projection=ccrs.GOOGLE_MERCATOR,
        tiles="CartoLight",
        #color="facecolor",   # per-row hex colors
        cmap="Blues",
        colorbar=True,
        hover_cols=["postcode", "stedelijkheid", sector],
        frame_width=900,
        frame_height=700,
    )
#create_map()

                                  options=dict(LABELS))

    create_map#, sector=sector_widget
)
#widgets = pn.Column(sector_widget, sizing_mode="fixed", width=300)
#pn.Column(widgets, bound_plot)#.show()
pn.template.MaterialTemplate(
    site="Panel",
    title="Getting Started App",
    #sidebar=[sector_widget],
    main=[bound_plot],
).servable(); # The ; is needed in the notebook to not display the template. Its not needed in a script

LABELS.keys()
dict_keys(['All Third Places', 'All Third Places except Medical Places', 'All Third Places except Medical and Retail Places', 'Medical Places only', 'Food and Beverage only', 'Retail Places only', 'Community Places only', 'Cultural Places only', 'Sport and Leisure only'])
for eachkey in list(LABELS.keys()):
    
    pn.template.MaterialTemplate(
        site="Third Places Dashboard",
        title=eachkey,
        #sidebar=[sector_widget],
        main=create_map(LABELS[eachkey]),
    ).save("../docs/maps/" + LABELS[eachkey] + ".html", resources=INLINE)

/Users/tillhovestadt/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Sociology/Kurse/SICSSS_Rotterdam/kroeg/scripts/panel_mapping.ipynb:69: FutureWarning: ChainedAssignmentError: behaviour will change in pandas 3.0!
You are setting values through chained assignment. Currently this works in certain cases, but when using Copy-on-Write (which will become the default behaviour in pandas 3.0) this will never work to update the original DataFrame or Series, because the intermediate object on which we are setting values will behave as a copy.
A typical example is when you are setting values in a column of a DataFrame, like:

df["col"][row_indexer] = value

Use `df.loc[row_indexer, "col"] = values` instead, to perform the assignment in a single step and ensure this keeps updating the original `df`.

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy

  "\tconst id_el = document.getElementById(root_id)\n",

A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  "\tconst id_el = document.getElementById(root_id)\n",

import numpy as np import matplotlib.colors as mcolors import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def blend_with_white(base_color, t): ““” t in [0, 1] 0 -> white 1 -> full base_color ““” base = np.array(mcolors.to_rgb(base_color)) white = np.array([1, 1, 1]) rgb = (1 - t) * white + t * base return mcolors.to_hex(rgb)

def create_map(sector=“third_places_total_ranked_by_urbanisation”): df = gdf.copy()

# one base color per category
cats = sorted(df["stedelijkheid"].dropna().unique())
palette = plt.get_cmap("tab10").colors
cat_to_color = {cat: palette[i % len(palette)] for i, cat in enumerate(cats)}

# normalize numeric sector to 0..1
s = df[sector].astype(float)
s_norm = (s - s.min()) / (s.max() - s.min() + 1e-9)

# combine category hue + numeric intensity
# low values -> lighter, high values -> stronger
df["facecolor"] = [
    blend_with_white(cat_to_color[cat], 0.25 + 0.75 * val)
    for cat, val in zip(df["stedelijkheid"], s_norm)
]

return df.hvplot.polygons(
    geo=True,
    crs=ccrs.epsg(28992),
    projection=ccrs.GOOGLE_MERCATOR,
    tiles="CartoLight",
    color="facecolor",   # per-row hex colors
    line_color="white",
    hover_cols=["postcode", "stedelijkheid", sector],
    frame_width=900,
    frame_height=700,
)
